workflows-reference.purpose: Provide Clean Architecture plus SOLID plus TDD workflows for Compass tasks.

documentation-context.rule: For every Compass-guided task, read the target project's available docs before planning or editing.
Use docs/decisions/0001-orientation-lock.md as the default architecture, principles, and workflow lock when it exists.
Read architecture, foundation, and process docs linked by the orientation lock before designing packages, tests, boundaries, or implementation flow.
Read relevant docs/modules notes before touching a module, domain, feature area, bounded context, or slice.
Read relevant docs/decisions records before changing boundaries, dependencies, data ownership, contracts, build flow, or release flow.
Read docs/reference when changing schemas, public contracts, naming rules, note structure, or orientation presets.
Do not require AGENTS.md to link or duplicate Compass docs; AGENTS.md is repository instruction context only when present.
If relevant docs are missing, continue with the best available evidence and report the documentation gap.
If clean-solid-tdd docs were just bootstrapped, adapt Go-oriented reference examples to the target language or stack before using them as project rules.

task-memory-gate.rule: For long or risky work with at least two concrete implementation slices after alignment or design, load Compass references/task-memory.xml.
Implementation may not start until the task memory outcome is reported as created, resumed, or not-required.
When task memory is required, docs/.tasks/<task>/goal.md, diagram.md, and memories.md must exist before the first implementation edit or command.
Missing docs/_templates task memory files are a documentation gap, not permission to skip this gate.

workflow.description: Add a new capability through three gated phases.

enforcement.rule: Run phases sequentially: brainstorming, then design, then implementation.
Execute exactly one feature per new_feature workflow.
If the user's request contains multiple features, create a TODO plan that lists all feature slices, select only one slice for the current workflow, and leave the rest pending.
Do not implement multiple features in one new_feature cycle, even when they belong to the same module or share adapters.
Brainstorming and design are planning-only phases; do not edit files, write tests, apply patches, or run implementation commands in those phases.
Design must produce a usecase-first contract, domain shape, and behavior-test matrix before implementation is allowed.
Implementation must run one small approved slice at a time, then stop for developer validation before the next slice.
Before implementation starts, satisfy the task-memory-gate for any long or risky multi-slice feature.
Stop after each developer_approval_required phase and wait for explicit developer approval before loading or executing the next phase.
A request to build a feature starts the workflow; it does not approve skipping brainstorming or design.
If the developer explicitly asks to skip a phase, state the skipped gate and risk before continuing.

phase.name: brainstorming
phase.purpose: Collaborate on the feature idea and produce a shared UX-level understanding before designing code.

required-docs.doc: orientation-linked architecture, foundation, and process docs

actions.action: Read the required docs and relevant module or decision docs before asking detailed feature questions.
Confirm that architecture and TDD docs are adapted to the selected or detected project language before design begins.
Do not edit files, create tests, run implementation commands, or apply patches in this phase.
Identify whether the request contains more than one feature; if it does, produce a TODO plan and choose only one feature slice for the current workflow.
Restate the user problem, target users, success criteria, and constraints.
Explore ideas according to project principles: start small, clarity over cleverness, one reason to change, protected boundaries, inward dependencies, and cheap verification.
Produce a UX output: user flow, API-facing behavior, screens or states if relevant, happy path, common path, and visible failure states.
Call out scope exclusions and risks before design begins.

phase-output.summary: What the agent understands from the brainstorming.
phase-output.todo-plan: When multiple features are detected, list all feature slices and mark only one as selected for the current workflow.
phase-output.ux-output: User-facing or API-facing behavior, flow, and states.
phase-output.principle-fit: How the idea follows the relevant docs.

pause-prompt.message: Stop and ask the developer to choose the next step.
pause-prompt.question: Should we run align-context before design?
Should we continue to the design phase?

routine.purpose: Challenge the shared understanding without relying on another skill.

instructions.instruction: Interview the developer relentlessly about every aspect of the selected feature plan until there is a shared understanding.
Walk down each branch of the design tree and resolve dependencies between decisions one by one.
Ask exactly one question at a time.
For each question, provide the agent's recommended answer and a short reason.
If a question can be answered by exploring the codebase or documentation, explore the codebase or documentation instead of asking the developer.
When exploration answers the question, report the evidence and continue to the next unresolved question.
Stop when remaining uncertainty no longer affects the selected feature's design, then summarize aligned decisions, open risks, and the next design input.

phase.name: design
phase.purpose: Design the selected feature's usecase, domain shape, and behavior-test coverage before creating adapters.

required-docs.doc: orientation-linked architecture, foundation, and process docs

actions.action: Read the required docs, orientation-linked docs, and relevant module or decision docs before designing packages, ports, tests, or data flow.
Do not edit files, create tests, run implementation commands, or apply patches in this phase; describe the test design instead.
Design only the selected feature slice. Keep other feature slices in the TODO plan as pending work.
Identify the relevant boundary and whether this is implementation growth inside the existing structure or an architecture change.
Start from the usecase contract: name, intent, input DTO, output DTO, success result, business errors, and consumer-side ports.
Design the domain shape: entities, value objects, invariants, policies, normalization rules, and domain errors owned by this feature.

Create a behavior-test matrix before implementation. Order scenarios from most common happy/common paths to validation failures, duplicates, missing dependencies, external failures, concurrency, permission, cross-module projection risks, and other edge cases relevant to this feature.
For each behavior scenario, specify the cheapest meaningful test level: domain for pure rules, usecase for orchestration, adapter/integration only for boundary mapping or persistence assumptions.
Mark each suggested behavior as selected, rejected, or postponed. Do not leave the behavior list ambiguous.
Challenge the design for gaps until there is no obvious unhandled common or edge behavior. If a gap remains, ask the developer before implementation.
Do not design delivery, persistence, memory, queue, or external adapters beyond the boundary expectations needed by the contract and behavior tests.

phase-output.usecase-contract: Usecase name, intent, inputs, outputs, business errors, ports, and dependency direction.
phase-output.domain-shape: Entities, value objects, invariants, policies, normalization rules, and domain errors.
phase-output.behavior-test-matrix: Ordered behavior scenarios from common to edge, with test level and expected result for each scenario.
phase-output.behavior-decisions: Selected, rejected, and postponed scenarios with rationale.
phase-output.knowledge-gaps: Show list of knowledge gaps which not find in hesitant/ambigous/codebase/context (don't show as questions)
phase-output.docs-impact: Docs or decision notes that must change.

pause-prompt.message: Stop and ask the developer to approve the design before implementation.
pause-prompt.question: Should any behavior scenario be added, removed, selected, rejected, or postponed?
Should we continue to implementation?

phase.name: implementation
phase.purpose: Implement the approved design through small verified slices, stopping for developer validation after each slice.

required-docs.doc: orientation-linked architecture, foundation, and process docs

actions.action: Read the required docs, orientation-linked docs, and relevant module or decision docs before editing files.
Confirm that brainstorming and design were approved before editing files.
Before the first implementation edit or command, report the task memory outcome: created, resumed, or not-required.
Implement only the selected feature slice from the approved design. Do not opportunistically implement pending TODO feature slices.
Before editing each implementation slice, state the slice name, exact behavior from the approved matrix, files likely to change, and verification to run.

Implement exactly one small slice per prompt. A slice should usually be one of: core behavior test and core code; one application behavior test and orchestration code; one technical adapter; one delivery adapter mapping; one persistence adapter or migration; one wiring change.
After each slice, run only the relevant verification for that slice, then stop and ask the developer for manual validation before continuing.
Do not continue from core behavior into adapters, or from one adapter into another adapter, without developer approval after the checkpoint.
Implement core behavior and application orchestration before adapters unless the approved slice explicitly starts with infrastructure.

Add adapters only when their boundary behavior needs evidence: delivery for request, response, and status mapping; persistence for mapping, query, and schema assumptions; memory for proof-of-concept or focused tests; technical adapters for concrete outside capabilities.
Keep interfaces on the consumer side and contracts narrow.
Keep composition code near the project's existing composition root.
For read-only projections across boundaries, keep the query outside the core and document the schema dependency with tests or migration evidence.
For rules or invariants owned by another boundary, stop and use an explicit contract or revisit the boundary. Do not duplicate another boundary's rule in persistence queries.
Add comments for why, assumptions, business rules, edge cases, or non-obvious decisions. Do not add mechanical comments.
Run the formatter and tests discovered from the project or documented by the repository. Use the cheapest sufficient verification, then broaden when risk or scope warrants it.

phase-output.next-slice-question: Ask whether the developer approves continuing to the next implementation slice.

workflow.evidence: Approved brainstorming output, approved contract and test design, failing-then-passing behavior test where practical, adapter evidence when adapters are added, touched-scope tests, docs update if contract or boundary changes.

workflow.description: Change an existing use case or rule by controlling the delta from protected baseline behavior.

enforcement.rule: Run phases sequentially: baseline-discovery, delta-design, implementation-checkpoints, and rollout-cleanup when rollout guards or compatibility paths are introduced.
Classify as architecture_change first when the update changes dependency direction, layer ownership, module structure, or cross-boundary contracts.
Before implementation starts, satisfy the task-memory-gate for any long, risky, or multi-slice update.
Keep core behavior independent of delivery, persistence, framework, and runtime infrastructure.
High-risk updates must define rollback, feature-flag, dark-launch, compatibility, or cleanup evidence before implementation.

phase.name: baseline-discovery
phase.purpose: Protect the current use case, contract, and regression surface before deciding what may change.

actions.action: Find the owning core rule, application use case, adapter, tests, docs, commands, and composition points.
State current behavior, public API or delivery contracts, persistence assumptions, external integrations, and expectations that must remain stable.
Classify update risk as minor, medium, or high based on blast radius, reversibility, data impact, user visibility, and boundary impact.
Record ambiguity before designing the delta.

phase-output.baseline: Owning core/application/adapter behavior, tests, docs, and verification commands observed.
phase-output.preserve-list: Behavior, contracts, invariants, and non-goals that must not drift.
phase-output.risk-level: minor, medium, or high with reason.

phase.name: delta-design
phase.purpose: Define the exact behavior change and the cheapest meaningful regression evidence before editing.

actions.action: State old behavior, new behavior, unchanged behavior, and intentionally removed behavior if any.
Define core, application, adapter, integration, or end-to-end tests for preserved and changed behavior at the cheapest sufficient level.
Define the smallest owning layer, module, use case, or adapter to change.
Decide whether the update needs a feature flag, dark launch, compatibility path, rollback plan, monitoring, docs update, or decision record.
Define the implementation slices and verification command for each slice.

phase-output.delta: Approved behavior change and unchanged behavior.
phase-output.rollout-risk: Flag, rollback, monitoring, compatibility, cleanup, or not needed.
phase-output.docs-impact: Docs or decisions to update, or none.

phase.name: implementation-checkpoints
phase.purpose: Apply one approved delta slice while keeping protected behavior covered.

actions.action: Before the first implementation edit or command, report the task memory outcome: created, resumed, or not-required.
Add or update the approved regression and changed-behavior tests for the current slice when practical.
Change the smallest owning core, application, adapter, or composition point and avoid introducing competing patterns.
Run the current slice verification command.
Stop after each verified slice and ask before continuing.

phase.name: rollout-cleanup
phase.purpose: Close temporary risk controls introduced for the update.

actions.action: Record feature flag, dark launch, compatibility path, monitoring, rollback, or cleanup status when any were introduced.
Remove stale flags or inactive compatibility paths after the update is fully accepted.
Update docs, decisions, public contracts, or runbooks when behavior or rollout expectations changed.
Run final verification for the touched scope.

phase-output.rollout-state: Flag, rollout, compatibility, rollback, or cleanup state.

workflow.evidence: Approved baseline, approved delta design, regression coverage for preserved behavior, changed-behavior verification, slice checkpoints, and rollout or cleanup evidence when needed.

steps.step: Reproduce or gather evidence for the bug.
Minimize the failing scenario.
Identify root cause before editing.
Add a failing regression test.
Fix the smallest cause.
Run targeted tests, then broader tests if risk warrants.

workflow.evidence: Reproduction, regression test, and passing verification.

steps.step: Stabilize first and reduce blast radius.
Identify the smallest safe fix.
Avoid broad refactors.
Verify the production-risk path.
Record follow-up work for root cause, tests, and cleanup.

workflow.evidence: Incident symptom addressed, minimal patch verified, follow-up noted.

steps.step: Define behavior that must remain unchanged.
Ensure tests cover the behavior before editing.
Refactor in small steps.
Run the same tests after each meaningful step.
Do not mix behavior changes into the refactor.

workflow.evidence: Same behavior tests pass before and after.

steps.step: Read current architecture and decision docs.
State the boundary or dependency change explicitly.
Compare 2-3 approaches and trade-offs.
Update architecture or decision docs before or with code.
Implement in small slices.
Verify dependency direction and tests.

workflow.evidence: Updated docs, tests, and dependency-boundary review.

steps.step: Establish baseline metric.
Identify bottleneck with evidence.
Optimize the narrow bottleneck.
Compare before and after.
Add benchmark or regression guard when practical.

workflow.evidence: Measured improvement and no behavior regression.

steps.step: Identify asset, threat, and affected boundary.
Avoid exposing secrets or sensitive data in logs, diffs, or final output.
Patch the smallest vulnerable path.
Add tests for authorization, validation, sanitization, or failure mode.
Consider dependency advisories if relevant.

workflow.evidence: Vulnerability path closed, sensitive data protected, tests pass.

steps.step: Identify schema owner and data ownership.
Decide whether the migration is additive, destructive, or data-moving.
Create migration under the owner scope.
Define rollback or forward-fix expectations.
Add integration tests for schema assumptions or query behavior.
Ensure cross-boundary read projections are explicit when present.

workflow.evidence: Migration path, rollback or forward plan, integration test or schema check.

steps.step: Read changelog or release notes when meaningful.
Identify compatibility risks.
Update dependency and lockfiles together.
Run tests and build commands.
Note manual follow-up if behavior changes are required.

workflow.evidence: Updated dependency files and compatibility verification.

steps.step: Reproduce the current command or CI behavior.
Change the smallest config or script.
Run the exact command locally when possible.
Document new command expectations if developer-facing.

workflow.evidence: Command output or reason it could not be run.

steps.step: Find the source-of-truth document.
Update the smallest relevant docs.
Avoid competing sources of truth.
Verify links, paths, taxonomy, and status.
State the risk the test protects.
Add focused tests without changing production behavior.
Avoid overfitting tests to private implementation details.
Run targeted tests.

workflow.evidence: Test command and unchanged production behavior.

steps.step: Keep scope narrow.
Avoid behavior, architecture, and unrelated formatting drift.
Run the cheapest verification.
Timebox the investigation.
Gather evidence from code, docs, internet or primary sources.
Compare options.
Recommend next action.
Avoid production code changes unless the user asks.

workflow.evidence: Findings, options, recommendation, and open questions.

steps.step: Check branch status and release prerequisites.
Verify tests, build, and package commands.
Prepare changelog, version, tag, or deploy steps as requested.
Avoid force-push or destructive release actions without explicit approval.
